title: Circular Magnetization
narrationScript: Run electric current through any conductor and a magnetic field appears around it the instant the current flows. No delay, no exceptions. The field forms closed circles around the current path, centered on the conductor and stacked along its whole length. That simple fact is the foundation of circular magnetization, the first of the two field directions every magnetic particle technician has to command. To know which way the flux circles, use the right-hand rule. Grip the conductor with your right hand so your thumb points in the direction of conventional current flow, from positive to negative. Your curled fingers now show the direction the magnetic field wraps around the conductor. Reverse the current and the field reverses with it. Strength follows two rules. The field gets stronger as amperage rises, in direct proportion. And it changes with position. Inside a solid conductor carrying current, the field is zero at the exact center and increases steadily out to the surface, where it peaks. Outside the conductor it falls off as you move away. So in a head shot, the strongest field sits right at the surface of the part, exactly where most service cracks start. Now make the conductor steel instead of copper. Steel is ferromagnetic, and its permeability multiplies the flux density enormously compared with what the same magnetizing force produces in air. Better still, in a uniform part the circular flux closes entirely inside the metal. A true circular field has no external poles, nothing for a compass or a pocket field indicator to sense. Remember that, because it changes how we verify the field. Direct induction means the part itself carries the magnetizing current. On a wet horizontal bench that is the head shot, the part clamped between two contact heads while current passes end to end and flux circles the circumference. Contact quality is everything. Dirty plates, loose clamping, or breaking contact while current flows will draw an arc and burn the part, and an arc burn can scrap an expensive component or become a crack starter. Clean the contacts, use the contact pads, and never open the circuit under load. Prods are the portable version of direct induction, two hand-held electrodes pressed onto the surface, with typical procedure values around ninety to one hundred twenty-five amperes per inch of prod spacing, and spacing commonly six to eight inches. Prods can leave arc strikes and contact marks, so many aerospace and finished-surface specifications prohibit them outright. Indirect induction keeps the current off the part. With a central conductor you run a bar or cable through the bore, and the current in the bar magnetizes the part around it. The field is strongest at the inside surface, which current through the part itself can never achieve, and there is no electrical contact, so there is no burn risk. On large bores, offset the conductor against the wall. Each shot then covers an arc of roughly four times the conductor diameter, so you rotate the part and overlap the shots until the full circumference is covered. The induced current method goes one step further and makes a ring-shaped part the secondary winding of a transformer, so current flows around the ring with no contact at all. That circumferential current sets up flux that crosses circumferential cracks, which makes it ideal for bearing races. What does a circular field find? Flux has to cross a discontinuity to force leakage out at the surface. Circular flux runs around the part, so it crosses flaws that run along the part, longitudinal seams, laps, and quench cracks parallel to the axis. Detectability is best when the flux crosses the flaw at between forty-five and ninety degrees. A crack lying parallel to the flux lines barely disturbs them and gives little or no indication. One shot covers one family of orientations, so full coverage always needs a second field roughly perpendicular to the first. Because the circular field stays inside the part, you verify it with tools at the surface. A pie gauge or a shim-type artificial flaw standard, taped flaw side toward the part, shows whether direction and strength are adequate while you apply particles. A Hall-effect gaussmeter reads the tangential surface field, and procedures commonly look for something on the order of thirty to sixty gauss. Head shot amperage itself is set by your written procedure, typically a few hundred amperes per inch of part diameter. Treat every number in this lesson as a typical procedure value. Your written procedure governs. Add it all up and circular magnetization gives you a strong, simple field for longitudinal flaws, with the central conductor handling bores and batches of small parts cleanly. Its costs are the arc-burn risk whenever current touches the part, and a field you cannot see from the outside, so you prove it with gauges and shims, never by assumption.
imagePrompt: A technician in safety glasses clamps a steel shaft between the copper contact heads of a wet horizontal magnetic particle bench while dark suspension drips from the part under bright shop lighting.

sections.heading: Current Makes a Field: The Straight Conductor
sections.body:
Pass current through any conductor and a magnetic field instantly surrounds it, forming **closed concentric circles** around the current path. The field direction comes from the **right-hand rule**: grasp the conductor with your right hand, thumb pointing in the direction of conventional current flow (positive to negative), and your curled fingers show the direction the flux circles. Reverse the current, and the field reverses.

Two position rules govern strength. **Inside a solid conductor**, the field is zero at the exact center and rises steadily to a **maximum at the surface**. **Outside** the conductor, the field weakens with distance. Strength is also **directly proportional to amperage** - double the current, double the magnetizing force.

Now swap the copper bar for a steel part. Steel is ferromagnetic, so its **permeability** multiplies the flux density far beyond what the same magnetizing force produces in air. And in a part of uniform section, the circular flux **closes entirely inside the metal**: a true circular field has **no external poles**. A compass or pocket field indicator held next to the part sees nothing - a fact that matters when we verify the field later in this lesson.

sections.caption: The right-hand rule gives the direction of the circular flux around a current-carrying conductor; field strength peaks at the conductor surface and falls off outside.

sections.heading: Direct Induction: Head Shots and Prods
sections.body:
**Direct magnetic induction** means the part itself carries the magnetizing current. On a wet horizontal bench this is the **head shot**: the part is clamped between two contact heads, current passes end to end through the part, and circular flux wraps the circumference along its full length. It is fast, repeatable, and puts the strongest field at the part surface.

The price of direct contact is **arc-burn risk**. Dirty contact plates, inadequate clamping pressure, or opening the circuit while current flows can draw an arc that locally melts the surface - enough to scrap a finished component or start a crack in service.

**Prods** are portable direct induction: two hand-held electrodes pressed onto the surface pass current through the local volume between them, giving a distorted circular field around the current path. Typical procedure values run on the order of **90-125 amperes per inch of prod spacing**, with spacing commonly **6-8 inches**. Because prods can leave arc strikes and contact marks, many aerospace and finished-part specifications **prohibit** them - always check the governing procedure before reaching for prod cables.

sections.title: Never break contact under current
sections.body: Arcing at heads or prods happens when contact is made or broken while current flows. **Energize only after firm contact is established, and de-energize before lifting off.** Keep contact pads clean and use lead or braided pads on finished surfaces. An arc burn is a rejectable defect you created - and a flash hazard to you.

sections.heading: Indirect Induction: Central Conductor and Induced Current
sections.body:
**Indirect magnetic induction** magnetizes the part without passing current through it. The workhorse is the **central conductor**: a copper bar or cable threaded through the bore carries the current, and its circular field magnetizes the surrounding part. Two advantages follow. The field is **strongest at the inside surface** - exactly where a head shot is weakest - so ID discontinuities show clearly. And with **no electrical contact**, there is no arc-burn risk, which is why bores, rings, nuts, and batches of washers stacked on one bar are central-conductor work.

When the bore is much larger than the conductor, **offset** the conductor against the bore wall. Each shot then gives adequate field over an arc of roughly **four times the conductor diameter** measured on the inside circumference, so you rotate the part between shots with about **10 percent overlap** until the full circumference is covered.

The **induced current** method makes a ring-shaped part the secondary of a transformer: a fluctuating flux through the ring's opening induces current flowing around the ring itself, with no contact at all. That circumferential current produces flux crossing **circumferential cracks** - ideal for bearing races and similar rings.

sections.caption: Direct induction (head shot) versus indirect induction (central conductor), showing where the field is strongest and the flaw orientations each setup reveals.

sections.heading: Indication Geometry: What a Circular Field Finds
sections.body:
Leakage flux - and therefore an indication - appears only where flux lines **cross** a discontinuity. Circular flux runs **around** the part, so it crosses discontinuities that run **along** the part: longitudinal seams, laps, and quench or grinding cracks parallel to the axis. These interrupt the flux at or near 90 degrees and produce sharp, strong indications.

Detectability holds up well for flaws oriented **45 to 90 degrees to the flux**; below about 45 degrees the response weakens, and a discontinuity lying **parallel to the flux lines** (a transverse crack, under circular magnetization) barely disturbs the field and may give no indication at all.

The practical rule: **the detectable flaw runs parallel to the current path**. Current along the axis means flux around the circumference, which means longitudinal flaws light up. One shot covers one family of orientations - complete coverage of an unknown part always requires a second magnetization roughly perpendicular to the first, which is the subject of the next lesson.

sections.heading: Field Strength and Field Detection
sections.body:
How much current is enough? Field strength tracks amperage directly, and written procedures set the value by geometry. For head shots, typical procedure values run on the order of **300-800 amperes per inch of part diameter**, with the lower end for simple high-permeability parts and the higher end for low-permeability alloys or when maximum sensitivity is needed. For prods, the 90-125 A per inch of spacing rule applies. These are **typical values - your written procedure governs**.

Because a true circular field has **no external poles**, you cannot confirm it with a compass-style field indicator held beside the part. Verification happens at the surface:

- **Pie (octagon) gauge** - a segmented disc laid on the surface during particle application; particles outline the segment joints when field direction and strength are usable.
- **Shim-type artificial flaw standards (QQIs)** - taped **flaw side toward the part**; the engraved flaw indicates when the field is adequate, in the orientation the shim is placed.
- **Hall-effect gaussmeter** - reads the tangential surface field; procedures commonly target on the order of **30-60 gauss** at the surface.

Prove the field - never assume it.

sections.caption: Circular magnetization techniques - advantages and disadvantages at a glance.
rows: Strong surface field along full length; fast and repeatable on bench parts
Arc-burn risk at contacts; weak at bore surfaces; finds longitudinal flaws only
Portable; local field on large weldments and castings
Arc strikes and contact marks; banned by many aerospace/finished-part specs; small coverage per placement
No contact, no burn risk; strongest field at ID; batches of small parts on one bar
Needs a bore or opening; offset shots must be rotated and overlapped (about 4x conductor diameter per shot)
No contact at all; finds circumferential cracks in rings and races
Limited to ring-shaped parts; special equipment

figures.title: Right-hand rule and field around a straight conductor

interactive.title: Pick the Circular Technique
interactive.intro: You are running the wet horizontal bench and a cart of mixed work arrives. For each part, choose the circular magnetization approach a Level II should choose - and prove the field before you call it inspected.

steps.prompt: A solid pump shaft with finished bearing journals is suspected of longitudinal quench cracks. How do you magnetize it?
options: Prods at 100 amperes per inch of spacing, walked along the shaft
Head shot between the bench contact heads with clean pads
Coil shot at the center of the shaft
steps.feedback: Longitudinal cracks need a circular field, which a head shot provides along the full length. Prods risk arc strikes on finished journals and are banned by many specs for exactly this kind of part. A coil gives a longitudinal field - wrong orientation for longitudinal cracks.

steps.prompt: Next is a hollow hydraulic cylinder body. The concern is axial (lengthwise) cracking on the inside diameter. Best setup?
options: Head shot - current through the cylinder wall
Central conductor bar through the bore
Yoke placed across the outside surface
steps.feedback: A central conductor puts the strongest circular field at the ID surface, exactly where the suspected axial cracks are, with no contact and no burn risk. A head shot concentrates field at the OD and is weak at the bore.

steps.prompt: A ring with a 12-inch bore goes on a 1.5-inch central conductor cable, offset against the bore wall. The inside circumference is about 38 inches. How do you cover it?
options: One shot - the field fills the whole ring regardless of offset
Two shots, one per side
At least seven overlapping shots, rotating the ring between shots
steps.feedback: An offset central conductor gives adequate field over roughly four times the conductor diameter - about 6 inches of circumference per shot here. Covering 38 inches with about 10 percent overlap takes at least seven rotated shots.

steps.prompt: Before inspecting, you must confirm the circular field in the shaft is adequate. What works?
options: Hold a pocket field indicator next to the part and watch for deflection
Tape a QQI shim flaw-side toward the surface and verify it indicates during the shot
Assume adequacy because the ammeter matched the procedure value
steps.feedback: A circular field is contained in the part - no external poles, so a field indicator beside the part shows nothing. A QQI (or pie gauge) on the surface proves both direction and adequacy. Ammeter alone does not prove field at the surface, though the reading is still recorded.

checkQuestions.question: Using the right-hand rule on a head shot where conventional current flows along the part axis, the magnetic flux:
options: Runs parallel to the current, along the axis
Circles the part circumference, perpendicular to the current
Points radially outward from the centerline
Forms poles at the two ends of the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Thumb along the current, fingers curl in the flux direction: the field forms closed circles around the current path, wrapping the circumference. Poles at the ends are a feature of longitudinal, not circular, magnetization.

checkQuestions.question: In a solid bar carrying magnetizing current, where is the field strength greatest?
options: At the exact center of the bar
Uniform everywhere in the cross-section
At the bar surface
Several inches away from the bar in air
checkQuestions.explanation: Field strength is zero at the center of a current-carrying conductor, rises to a maximum at the surface, and falls off with distance outside - one reason direct circular magnetization favors surface flaw detection.

checkQuestions.question: A weld is to be prod-magnetized with the electrodes spaced 7 inches apart. Using typical procedure values of 90-125 amperes per inch of spacing, an appropriate current is about:
options: 125 amperes
700 amperes
1,800 amperes
90 amperes
checkQuestions.explanation: At 90-125 A per inch and 7 inches of spacing, the window is roughly 630-875 amperes, so about 700 A fits. Always confirm against the written procedure.

checkQuestions.question: A 2-inch diameter central conductor is offset against the bore wall of a large ring. Roughly how much of the inside circumference receives adequate field per shot?
options: About 2 inches
About 8 inches
The entire circumference
About 24 inches
checkQuestions.explanation: The offset central conductor rule of thumb is adequate field over about four times the conductor diameter - here roughly 8 inches - so the part must be rotated through overlapping shots for full coverage.

checkQuestions.question: Why can't a pocket field indicator held beside a circularly magnetized bar confirm the field?
options: The field alternates too quickly for the indicator to follow
A true circular field is contained within the part and has no external poles
Circular fields are too weak to detect with any instrument
The indicator only responds to fields from AC current
checkQuestions.explanation: Circular flux closes entirely inside a uniform part, so there is no external leakage for the indicator to sense; verification uses a pie gauge, QQI shim, or Hall-effect gaussmeter at the surface.

references: ASTM E709 (guide to magnetic particle testing - general practice)
ASTM E1444/E1444M (magnetizing technique and verification - general practice)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (prod technique and amperage - general practice)
